speaker: interviewer
text: And you can pick the ones that you want to enumerate.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think this has been-- right. This is Blizyn actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Horn, you were talking about being sent to Blizyn-- Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to set up a new concentration camp with 80 other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: 79 Other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Blizyn was a-- a labor camp. And it was-- it was set up in March of 1943. The main reason for the existence of Blizyn there, for building up Blizyn was to produce army goods for the German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Blizyn was set up to produce army uniforms, army hats, army sweaters, army socks. And factories were put together in the camp to produce that. And most of the people who had come to Blizyn, people from all over the general government, all over Poland, people from cities like Piotrkow, Czestochowa, Tomaszow, Kielce, Bialystok, from these great cities-- each city sent a contingent of people, of Jews, who had been, similarly to Radom, left alive, because they were trade-- they were people with a trade. They were people who knew how to produce things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so Blizyn was a meat grinder, you know. People would not last long. The rations were starvation rations, and people would die quickly. But as soon as people died, they replaced them with others, and they kept sending them in. One of the people that came into Blizyn was a boy named Jacob. Jacob came from Czestochowa. And Jacob wound up bunking right next to me. And one day-- one night, as we were laying next to each other, he told me this terrible secret that he carried with himself. And I want to read back out of my memoirs that which Jacob had told me. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] 'Not long after, new transports began to arrive in Blizyn. And among them was a boy named Jacob. He came from Czestochowa and was assigned to my barrack. He was an attractive boy with dark, brooding features. I became friends with him and he eventually bunked next to me. After lights out, we would share our memories of home and family, and of days gone by. One night, he shared a terrible secret with me. Jacob, along with some members of his family, had been deported from Czestochowa to Treblinka in the summer of 1942. When they arrived, about half the people of his car were dead from heat and lack of water. The rest were unloaded on the ramp and chased with whips and dogs to a field, where they had to undress. At that point, an assessment picked him and a few other healthy-looking young boys and told them to step out. They were taken to join the group that serviced the Treblinka death camp facility. The rest of the people were sent to a sealed barrack, gassed, and cremated. It was Jacob that introduced me for the first time to the horrible truth at the heart of the Holocaust, that death was not a byproduct of the camp. Death was the reason the camp existed. The whole camp system was a swift and efficient genocide machine conceived and built by this German super-race of psychopaths. He taught me a song which the slaves in Treblinka sang in the barracks at night after work. I only recall a fraction of the lyrics, but I will never forget the haunting melody. The lyrics are 'Treblinka there, for every Jew a final resting place. Whoever goes there remains there, remains forever. Whoever gets there, our mothers, fathers, sisters, brothers, they're all poisoned and that is their end.' After Jacob had learned the truth, he escaped from Treblinka by burying himself in a truck full of clothing and other loot taken from the victims, and made his way back to Czestochowa where he still had close family. When he arrived there, he told the others what he had seen. No one believed him, not even his own father. Out of anger and frustration, Jacob volunteered for Blizyn. He stopped telling the story, except to people like me, whom he befriended and liked. He said I was one of the very few who believed him.' [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This was my introduction to the real purpose, to the real thing of the Holocaust. After I heard the story from Jacob, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could no longer delude myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything fell into place, you might say, because what I'd witnessed and what I saw, even at this tender age of 16 or 15 at the time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just understood that this is the way it had to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they take so many men, women, children? Where would they take them, to the Ukraine, to till lands? What lands? How could they till lands? Where would they be there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And actually that period was also one of the worst in my life, because at that period, I began to say Kaddish after my parents, because that was a time when I really-- when I really believed that they were dead. Up until that time, I still deluded myself that it's possible they may be alive. So that was a turning point in my life in the camps. And I knew then that I was probably all alone on the world at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it give you a stronger desire to survive after what Jacob told you and you began saying Kaddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at first. I tell you, what gave me the desire to survive was something else. This camp was-- as I said, it's a terrible place and epidemics would break out periodically. And one of the epidemics I fell victim to, a typhus epidemic, and I got sick. And after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming down with the sickness, you know, maybe one out of three survived once you contracted the sickness. But I made it through because of help of some people who helped me. And I remember there was a fellow by the name of Saul Mincberg, who was one of the inmate administrators of the camp, who helped me after my sickness. And for some reason, he was very good to me. And this human exchange, you know, and the fact that not everybody is so bad, that gave me hope. And it helped a great deal. And he brought me in, and he gave me another job in Blizyn. And he helped me out. He took me to the kitchen after my sickness. And he told the cook to give me extra rations every day until I recovered. It was things like this in the camp that a young man of-- like of my age was able to start life anew, get new hope, and get going again, and face whatever was coming one more time. We stayed in Blizyn until the summer of 1944. The summer of 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans begin to evacuate the area, because the Russian armies were coming close on the eastern side. And in the summer of 1944, we were taken-- one morning we were taken to the rail station in Blizyn and loaded into freight cars. And I remember going over to one of the mesh pockets of the rail car. And I actually opened it up, took out the bill of lading. And on the bill of lading I saw clearly marked Auschwitz. So from Blizyn, we went to Auschwitz. And Auschwitz began this new era for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Saul Mincberg with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Saul Mincberg remained in the camp. He was one of the 50 that remained to clean up. You know the Germans never left-- left abruptly everything. They were very thorough, well-organized, very efficient. They left 50 people to clean up and to dismantle whatever they needed. So Saul was among those, but I met him later. We came into Auschwitz in the summer of 1944. I think it was either the end of June or in July. And a new saga began for me. Because of certain connections and of a friend of mine who found a-- a boyhood friend of his working in the sauna, which was some kind of a privileged job in Auschwitz, we were given the opportunity to go on a work kommando that was-- that worked in a close proximity to the arrival ramp where the people came into Auschwitz arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked on the arrival ramp for several months. And for several months, daily I witnessed the many, many, many transports that came in from all over Europe, transports that came in from Western Europe, Holland, Belgium, France, and also from the Lodz ghetto, which was being emptied out at that time. And most of them came in from Hungary. That was all in the period when I worked at the-- The summer of '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Summer of '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that work on the ramp at Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was probably the closest that any onlooker has come to see the Holocaust in action without being directly involved in it, because I worked on a work unit which was charged with building something on the ramp. We were building a-- a hole in the ground, which later-- which we knew to be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they later filled it with water. It was supposed to be a fire prevention thing. And so I was able for these several months-- the key months in Auschwitz, I was able to be on the ramp and witness all of the deportations. The thing that I talked to you about, Chaim Rumkowski who arrived at that particular time, I witnessed. I witnessed exactly and I remember the day very well. When he arrived, there was a commotion on the ramp. I saw these several SS officers came over to this white-haired Jew. And they seemed to be giving him a lot of attention. And of course, we asked the others who that was. And they told us that he is Chaim Rumkowski, who was at that point called the King of the Jews. Well, I saw them, the SS talking to him very amiably, you know. And as they did, they walked with him. And as they talked, they walked towards the crematorium. And the last I saw of him is they walked him right into the gas chamber. And that was the end of the Chaim Rumkowski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also saw many other things which are a little hard to describe. There was one train I remember that came in that consisted of World War I Jewish veterans. Most of them had the iron cross decorations on them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was eerie, you know, even for those days, to see people get off a train, which was like private Pullman cars, brought in in-- in excellent condition. People get off this train. Some of them were in wheelchairs. And some of them had the crosses hanging down on their chest. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I saw them all-- some of them went in the Red Cross cars. Some of them went-- walked in. Some of them were pushed in. Everything went in there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. I need to live many years to be able to describe what I saw on that ramp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in Auschwitz-- I lived through-- by the way, I lived through the revolt in Auschwitz, which happened on September 7th. I was in very close proximity to the crematorium number 3 and 4 when they blew the-- when-- when the revolt occurred. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, the guards that were with us made us lay down, and we had to bury our head in the ground until the whole thing was over, so I-- I didn't actually see. So when we got up, we saw a few SS guards were laying dead on the ground near the crematoria. And that was over. The revolt was over for us. We had some anxious days later on. They threatened to kill a lot. And then we had a series of hangings after that, where almost every Sunday they would take out dozens of people, you know, and line them up and hang them in view-- full view of everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to mention another thing about Auschwitz which I think is important. Most Sundays, hangings took place in the main square of Camp D in Auschwitz, where I was stationed. After the hangings, they had a tremendous orchestra, with probably the best musicians in Europe at the time, because as the-- as the trains arrived, the first questions that the SS asked, are there any musicians here? And they would pluck them out. So they had accumulated probably the best musicians in the world. And they had an orchestra, which comprised at least 50 to 60 men playing. And these people were playing every Sunday. They gave concerts after the hangings. In their uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: In their-- you mean in their stripes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In their uniforms. There was a lectern. There was an orchestra leader. And I remember there was a cordoned-off section for the Germans, for those who were not inmates who came to listen to the concerts. And I know for a fact that the concert couldn't start until the town church let out. There was a time when the town church let out. And they had to give these Germans who attended church time to make it the camp so they could listen to the concert. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you understand the implication? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am turned off to classical music. And I believe I'm turned off to classical music because I constantly associate classical music with these Germans who came to listen to the concert, even from the churches, and enjoyed it so much at a place like Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In November of 1947-- May I ask, would it be-- was it your opinion at that time that the townspeople knew what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody knew what was going on. Everybody knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: On any of your work missions did you ever have any contact with townpeople?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. As a matter of fact, on one of the jobs that I worked, there were Swiss people who were working in Auschwitz, in Birkenau, on a contract--

speaker: interviewee
text: To build a--

speaker: interviewee
text: To build barracks. OK? They-- a Swiss firm had bid on a contract to build barracks in Auschwitz. And these were civil engineers that were sent by their firms. And they used the slave labor from Auschwitz to do their work. Now, don't tell me they didn't know what Auschwitz was about. Of course they did. Everybody did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1944, none of this was a secret. In '42, perhaps it was. But not 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask were-- were you ever tattooed in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. When I arrived, I was tattooed. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your number?

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, would you repeat how old you were when this happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944. That makes me 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were discussing your feelings and anger, resentment to the SS who were watching the concerts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who were listening to concerts. Well, the resentment stems primarily from the fact that we were made to watch the hangings. The hangings, to watch the hangings was just an awful ordeal. And they would chase us all out of the barracks. The barrack had to be clean. If they caught anybody in the barrack, they would kill them. Everybody had to go out and join the square to watch the hangings. And they would take like 20 people. And they had the gallows. And they would take one and put him on the gallows and hang him. And the other one would not-- they wouldn't stop until he stopped twitching. They would take him off and take the next one. Or if they had three or four gallows, they would do it three or four at a time. And the others were just waiting in line. They're waiting their turn. I-- I don't know that I ever saw anything more cruel than that. The people had been broken in the first place, because at the time, most of the hangings were for escape attempts. And a lot of the hangings that I watched were for people who were involved in the uprising that I described. After the uprising, they caught a lot of the sonderkommando that had escaped, that they made it out of the camp, and then they caught them. They caught most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sonderkommando, which are the people that worked in the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were the ones who started or participated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They were the ones who started--

speaker: interviewer
text: The uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the ones who started the uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: September '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was September-- the beginning of September '44, yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a period where I lived in a haze. Fortunately, because of the fact that I worked near the ramp, I was not hungry. I was able to get food.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people who kept coming in brought a lot of food with them. And some of that food made its way back outside. So the usual comment was-- the Polish boys who worked with us, the Russian boys who worked with us on that kommando, they would usually comment if-- if-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if a trainload arrived from Hungary, they would say tomorrow we eat Hungarian salami. OK? That was the world. That was it. The other thing that I recall very well, talking about religion again, the Gentile boys that worked with me on that kommando, who witnessed what I witnessed, you know, the arrival and the whole procedure, the gassing, and all that, they were totally convinced that this terrible misfortune that had befallen the Jews happened because of what the priest told them in the Catholic churches, that the Jews are getting it because they rejected Christ. They were so convinced on that point that, you know, I myself remember as a 17-year-old, I didn't have a good argument to use against them, because it seemed at the time that was so plausible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, they were-- they were killing Poles, and they were killing Russians, and-- and their-- and their lives were almost as bad as ours. But they were not-- they were not-- there was no genocide of Poles. There was no genocide of Russians. And they were not sending them into the crematoria. They were not sending them into the gas chamber. That was reserved primarily for Jews and, in a few instances, for Gypsies. But if you're a young man of 17 at the time-- and we talked about that many times. They took it as a matter of fact. They took it as a gospel that the Jews are being punished for rejecting Christ. There was absolutely no question in their minds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started to think it was true, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't start to think it was true. My Jewishness was too inculcated in me. But I remember that I-- I had no argument to use against it. I couldn't argue the point even if I wanted to, because it just seemed to make sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any camaraderie between the Jewish inmates and the Gentile ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: To some extent, yes. In Auschwitz, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were both persecuted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to a point, you know. There-- there many Poles who were real bad, they would carry this into the camp as well. But I would say that in most cases, there was more camaraderie in the camps than outside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So proceeding with these hangings, what-- what was the next biggest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --change in your-- your life at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz was the culmination of all my experiences. And I remember when I left Auschwitz one day in November when they evacuated us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my great dream and desire was to survive, to be a witness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the greatest desire I had, was to survive and be a witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: For your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: For my family, for my friends, and for what I saw in Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and what is indelibly marked in my-- in my brain. If I live to be 1,000, some of the things that I saw, I remember as if I saw them yesterday. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was one point where a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] troop-- a train came in from Hungary. And a lot of Hungarian people came out and they were in the Jewish garb. They were religious Orthodox people. And a lot of them walked from the ramp into the gas chamber with the prayer books wide open and praying and shaking, praying and shaking. And I remember we were not allowed to speak to them, even though they passed us by within a few-- they passed us within a few feet. And I remember one time there was a very learned-looking-- I would say a rabbi, beard, with an open book. And for some reason, they stopped. There was something going on ahead and they stopped for a while. And he looked at me. And he looked at me. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're a Jew. And I said nothing, because we weren't allowed to talk to them. And I was afraid to talk to them. And then if I did, what was I going to tell him? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But he insisted. And he said to me, are we going al Kiddush Hashem? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You understand what that means. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was afraid to answer and I couldn't say anything and I didn't want to tell him. But I remember saying in Yiddish, it would be a good time, Rabbi, for the Messiah to come right now. This is what I said to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had the order to march on and he marched on [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to oblivion. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted me to remind you about Steven Spielberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. I thought we would do that--
